title: Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand and the outbreak of the First World War
summary: On 28 June 1914 a nineteen-year-old Bosnian Serb named Gavrilo Princip shot the heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne, Archduke Franz Ferdinand, and his wife Sophie in a Sarajevo street. Within five weeks the alliance systems of Europe had turned the Balkan quarrel into a continental war. By early August the great powers were mobilising millions of men, German armies were driving through Belgium into France, and the century's first industrial-scale conflict had begun. The First World War would kill some ten million soldiers and reshape almost every state that took part.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Balkan crisis into which Archduke Franz Ferdinand rode on 28 June 1914 had been building for a generation. In 1908 the Austro-Hungarian government had formally annexed Bosnia and Herzegovina, provinces it had occupied since 1878 under the Congress of Berlin. The annexation infuriated the kingdom of Serbia, which regarded the Slavic populations of Bosnia as fellow South Slavs whose future lay with Belgrade. The two Balkan wars of 1912 and 1913 further inflamed the region: a coalition of small Christian states drove the Ottomans almost out of Europe, then fell out over the spoils, and Serbia emerged nearly doubled in size, self-confident, and menaced by Habsburg pressure to keep out of Albania.

Behind the Balkan quarrel stood the alliance system that Bismarck's diplomacy had bequeathed to Europe and that his successors had allowed to harden. Germany and Austria-Hungary had been bound by the Dual Alliance since 1879; Italy nominally joined them as the Triple Alliance in 1882. On the other side stood the Franco-Russian alliance of 1894 and the looser Entente Cordiale between Britain and France of 1904, extended to Russia in 1907. A naval arms race between Britain and Germany, colonial disputes over Morocco, and Russian and German plans for railway building through the Ottoman Empire had raised the temperature further.

Franz Ferdinand, nephew of the aged Emperor Franz Joseph, was heir presumptive to the Habsburg throne and inspector general of the imperial armed forces. In private he favoured a cautious foreign policy and a reorganisation of the monarchy that would give the Slavs a status equal to the Germans and Hungarians, a plan Serbian nationalists regarded as an existential threat to their project of a South Slav state under Belgrade. In 1914 the archduke agreed to attend Austro-Hungarian army manoeuvres in Bosnia and then to visit Sarajevo on Vidovdan, the anniversary of the medieval Serb defeat by the Ottomans at Kosovo in 1389, a symbolically charged date. His visit was known well in advance and offered a target of opportunity to a small conspiratorial group of young Bosnian Serbs armed and trained across the border by officers of the Serbian military intelligence service, most notably Colonel Dragutin Dimitrijevic, known as Apis, head of the secret society Union or Death, popularly called the Black Hand.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
Franz Ferdinand and his wife Sophie, Duchess of Hohenberg, arrived at Sarajevo railway station shortly before ten on the morning of Sunday 28 June 1914 and were driven in an open car along the Appel Quay beside the Miljacka river towards the city hall. Six young assassins recruited by the Black Hand agent Danilo Ilic were spaced along the route, armed with pistols and hand grenades. The first two lost their nerve. The third, Nedeljko Cabrinovic, threw a grenade that bounced off the folded roof of the archduke's car and exploded under the following vehicle, wounding officers and bystanders. Cabrinovic swallowed a cyanide capsule that only made him vomit and jumped into the shallow river, where he was seized.

The motorcade sped on to the town hall. After a brief and awkward reception the archduke insisted on visiting the wounded officers in hospital. His driver, given no clear new instructions, turned right off the quay into Franz Josef Street and, told he had taken the wrong route, stopped to reverse. By coincidence Gavrilo Princip, one of the earlier assassins who had drifted disconsolately away, was standing a few metres from the stalled car. He stepped forward, drew a Belgian-made FN Model 1910 pistol, and fired twice. One bullet struck the archduke in the neck; the other hit Sophie in the abdomen. Both were dead within the hour.

The response in Vienna was cold and calculating. Foreign minister Count Leopold Berchtold and chief of staff Franz Conrad von Hoetzendorf had long favoured a preventive war against Serbia; the emperor was persuaded, and Germany, urged on by Kaiser Wilhelm II, gave the so-called blank cheque of unconditional support on 5-6 July. On 23 July Vienna delivered an ultimatum designed to be rejected. Belgrade accepted nearly all its terms but Austria-Hungary declared war on 28 July and shelled Belgrade the next day. Russia ordered general mobilisation on 30 July in defence of Serbia. Germany declared war on Russia on 1 August and on France on 3 August, invading neutral Belgium the same day under the Schlieffen plan; Britain declared war on Germany on 4 August.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The war that followed was of a scale and duration that no capital had planned for. The German advance through Belgium was checked at the First Battle of the Marne in September 1914; by the end of the year a system of trenches ran from the North Sea to the Swiss border, and the western front had begun to consume men and munitions at unprecedented rates. In the east, Russian armies won early victories against Austria-Hungary in Galicia but were shattered by the Germans at Tannenberg. The Ottoman Empire joined the Central Powers in November 1914 and Italy the Entente in May 1915. Battles at Verdun and the Somme in 1916 alone produced roughly a million casualties each.

The United States, which had stayed out through the first two and a half years, entered on the Allied side in April 1917, tipping the eventual balance. The Bolshevik seizure of power in Russia that autumn took Petrograd out of the war by the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk in March 1918, but a final German offensive in the west failed by summer and, with mutinies at home and the collapse of Austria-Hungary and the Ottomans, Berlin signed the armistice of 11 November 1918 in a railway carriage at Compiegne.

By the war's end four empires, the German, Austro-Hungarian, Russian, and Ottoman, had ceased to exist. Some ten million soldiers were dead and at least six million civilians; the influenza pandemic of 1918-19, which spread along military supply lines, killed tens of millions more. The Paris peace conference of 1919 redrew the map of Europe and the Middle East, punished Germany with reparations and territorial losses under the Treaty of Versailles, and created the League of Nations, an experiment in collective security that would fail within a generation.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The war that began at Sarajevo is often called, with reason, the primal catastrophe of the twentieth century. It broke the confidence of European liberal civilisation, killed a generation of young men whose absence is legible in the cenotaphs of every French village and Commonwealth town hall, and left in place the grievances that Adolf Hitler would exploit two decades later. Its territorial settlements produced states, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia, Poland, Iraq, that would themselves fracture violently later in the century. Mandates over former Ottoman territories laid the foundations for the Israel-Palestine question and much of the political geography of the modern Middle East.

Militarily the war introduced or industrialised weapons that would dominate the century: the machine gun, poison gas, the submarine, the tank, and the aeroplane in combat use. Psychologically it introduced shell shock, later post-traumatic stress disorder, into medical vocabulary. It also transformed the role of the state, which now conscripted, rationed, taxed, and propagandised its citizens on a scale that would be normalised in the decades to follow. Women's mass entry into wartime work accelerated the extension of the franchise in Britain, Germany, and elsewhere.

The assassins themselves were mostly dead within four years. Princip, too young for the death penalty, was sentenced to twenty years and died of tuberculosis in the Habsburg fortress of Theresienstadt in April 1918. His bridge over the Miljacka has been renamed several times over the century as regimes have changed. The war he ignited did not, in the end, destroy the idea of Yugoslavia, nor of a united Europe; but it made both immeasurably harder to build.

worldContext.text: China in 1914 was three years into its republic. President Yuan Shikai, a former Qing general who had forced the abdication of the last emperor Puyi in 1912, was consolidating personal power at the expense of the parliament and the Nationalist Party (Kuomintang) of Sun Yat-sen. In August Japan, seizing the wartime distraction of the European powers, declared war on Germany and moved to occupy the German leased territory of Qingdao and the German-held Pacific islands north of the equator, prefiguring the Twenty-One Demands it would impose on Beijing the following year.

British India, a Crown possession of some 315 million people, was quietly mobilising in the summer of 1914. Within weeks of Britain's declaration of war Indian troops would be sailing for France, East Africa, and Mesopotamia; more than a million would ultimately serve. The Indian National Congress, led by moderates such as Gopal Krishna Gokhale, pledged loyalty in the expectation of postwar reform. A young lawyer named Mohandas Gandhi, whose civil disobedience campaign against racial laws in South Africa had just ended in compromise, sailed for London in July and would reach India in January 1915.

The Ottoman Empire in 1914 was ruled by the Committee of Union and Progress, dominated after early 1913 by the triumvirate of Enver, Talaat, and Cemal Pasha. Battered by the Balkan wars, the empire was rebuilding its army with German advisers under General Otto Liman von Sanders. On 2 August, the day after Germany declared war on Russia, Enver signed a secret Ottoman-German alliance; by November the empire was at war with Britain, France, and Russia, opening fronts from the Caucasus to Sinai and drawing the entire eastern Mediterranean into the conflict.

Almost the whole continent, apart from Ethiopia and Liberia, was ruled from Europe in 1914 under the borders drawn at the 1884-85 Berlin Conference. Within days of the European declarations, Allied troops from British and French African colonies invaded German Togoland, Kamerun, and later German South-West Africa and German East Africa, opening a set of little-remembered campaigns fought largely with African askari soldiers and porters. In the Union of South Africa, Prime Minister Louis Botha would face a rebellion by Boer veterans opposed to joining Britain's war against Germany.

The United States under President Woodrow Wilson had opened the Panama Canal to shipping on 15 August 1914 and proclaimed neutrality in the European war. Mexico was in the middle of its revolution: the dictator Victoriano Huerta resigned on 15 July under pressure from Constitutionalist armies under Venustiano Carranza and Pancho Villa, while American marines had occupied the port of Veracruz since April in a separate dispute. In Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and Newfoundland, dominion governments were already committing expeditionary forces to Britain's cause.

The Pacific dominions moved with startling speed. Within a month of the outbreak Australian and New Zealand expeditionary forces had seized German New Guinea, the Bismarck Archipelago, and German Samoa; Japan took the Marshall, Caroline, and Mariana Islands. The Australian Naval and Military Expeditionary Force landed at Rabaul in September 1914. A larger Australian Imperial Force and the New Zealand Expeditionary Force embarked for Egypt in November, from where they would sail to Gallipoli in April 1915, forging the ANZAC legend at heavy cost.

figures.name: Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria
figures.role: Heir presumptive to the Austro-Hungarian throne, assassinated at Sarajevo

figures.name: Sophie, Duchess of Hohenberg
figures.role: Wife of Franz Ferdinand, killed beside him in the second shot fired by Princip

figures.name: Gavrilo Princip
figures.role: Nineteen-year-old Bosnian Serb assassin, member of the Young Bosnia movement supplied by the Serbian Black Hand

figures.name: Emperor Franz Joseph I
figures.role: Aged Habsburg emperor who approved the ultimatum to Serbia

figures.name: Kaiser Wilhelm II
figures.role: German emperor who issued the 'blank cheque' of support to Vienna

figures.name: Tsar Nicholas II
figures.role: Russian emperor whose order for general mobilisation on 30 July 1914 helped trigger the general European war

images.caption: Archduke Franz Ferdinand and Duchess Sophie leaving the Sarajevo city hall shortly before their assassination on 28 June 1914.
images.credit: Karl Tröstl, published in Das Interessante Blatt, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: A man widely identified as Gavrilo Princip being seized by Sarajevo police moments after firing the fatal shots.
Crowds reading the announcement of German mobilisation in Berlin on 1 August 1914.

sources.title: The Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand
